claims.claim_text: The opening retail-platform production failure anecdote is presented as a real incident but has no attributable source.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters, paragraph 1
claims.anchor_text: A retail ML platform team once shipped a recommendation model that passed every notebook experiment, every unit test, and every offline evaluation gate. The model worked on the lead engineer's workstation, worked inside the training notebook, and worked during a short smoke test on a staging VM. Then production traffic arrived, the service began restarting, conversion metrics dropped, and the incident channel filled with people asking why a model that had already been "validated" could not survive real requests.
claims.suggested_rewrite: A retail ML platform team can ship a recommendation model that passes notebook experiments, unit tests, and offline evaluation, yet still fail under production traffic when the runtime environment differs from development and staging.
claims.rationale: This reads as a factual war story but provides no named incident or source, so it should be rewritten as a general operational lesson.

claims.claim_text: The follow-on paragraph gives incident-specific environmental details without an attributable source.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters, paragraph 2
claims.anchor_text: The failure was not a bad algorithm. The service had been trained and tested with one set of operating system libraries, built in CI with another base image, and deployed onto hosts with a different CUDA driver boundary. One missing OpenMP runtime only appeared when NumPy used parallel execution under load, and the production container had no readiness check capable of detecting the broken path before traffic arrived. The model was treated as the risky part, but the environment was the part that failed first.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Failures like this are often environmental rather than algorithmic: training, CI, and production can differ in base images, native libraries, CUDA boundaries, and readiness behavior, so a service may pass offline checks and still fail when real traffic exercises a missing runtime dependency.
claims.rationale: The operational lesson is sound, but the paragraph is written as a specific incident without a citable attribution.

claims.claim_text: Docker processes Dockerfile instructions in order, and once cache is invalidated, all subsequent instructions rebuild.
claims.span_hint: section: 2. Images Are Layered, opening paragraphs
claims.rationale: Docker's cache invalidation docs explicitly state that instructions are evaluated in order and that later commands rebuild after invalidation.

claims.claim_text: Multi-stage builds let you copy only selected artifacts into the final image and leave build tools behind.
claims.span_hint: section: 2. Images Are Layered, builder/runtime discussion
claims.rationale: Docker's multi-stage build docs describe copying chosen artifacts from earlier stages into a lean final stage.

claims.claim_text: A `.dockerignore` file removes matched files from the build context before the context is sent to the builder.
claims.rationale: Docker's build-context docs explicitly say `.dockerignore` exclusions are removed from the context before it is sent.

claims.claim_text: Liveness checks determine whether a container should be restarted, while readiness checks determine whether it should receive traffic.
claims.span_hint: section: 3. Debugging Environment Drift, health-check paragraphs
claims.rationale: Kubernetes probe docs directly define liveness as restart-oriented and readiness as traffic-gating.

claims.claim_text: Running a CUDA container requires a compatible host NVIDIA driver, while the host does not need the CUDA toolkit installed.
claims.rationale: The primary NVIDIA Container Toolkit documentation directly supports this, but `docs.nvidia.com` is off the current allowlist.

claims.claim_text: Newer NVIDIA drivers are backward-compatible with older CUDA toolkit versions, but each newer CUDA version requires a minimum driver version.
claims.span_hint: section: 4. GPU Containers, compatibility rule paragraph
claims.rationale: NVIDIA's CUDA compatibility documentation is the right primary source, but its host is not allowlisted.

claims.claim_text: Docker uses `64m` as the default `/dev/shm` size when `--shm-size` is not set.
claims.rationale: Docker's `docker run` reference states the default `/dev/shm` size is `64m`.

claims.claim_text: With `num_workers > 0`, PyTorch uses `/dev/shm`, and insufficient shared memory can trigger bus-error or shared-memory failures.
claims.span_hint: section: 4. GPU Containers, shared-memory paragraph; Did You Know #3; Quiz #4
claims.rationale: The PyTorch tutorial directly describes `/dev/shm` use and the associated worker bus errors, but `docs.pytorch.org` is off allowlist.

claims.claim_text: Named Docker volumes persist beyond the lifecycle of a container and are not deleted just because the container is removed.
claims.span_hint: section: 5. Model Artifacts, volume-mount paragraphs; Did You Know #4
claims.rationale: Docker's volume docs explicitly state that volume contents exist outside the lifecycle of a given container.

claims.claim_text: By default, `docker compose down` does not remove named volumes declared in the Compose file.
claims.span_hint: section: Hands-On Exercise, Task 3 success criteria
claims.rationale: Docker's Compose tutorial explicitly warns that named volumes are not removed by default on `docker compose down`.

claims.claim_text: Compose gives each service an internal DNS name equal to the service name, so containers should use service names instead of `127.0.0.1` for container-to-container calls.
claims.span_hint: section: 6. Docker Compose for Local ML Systems, networking paragraph
claims.rationale: Docker's Compose networking docs say each service registers its name with internal DNS for inter-container reachability.

claims.claim_text: Base image tags are mutable over time, so pinning versions or digests improves rebuild reproducibility.
claims.span_hint: section: 7. Production Readiness, pinning tags paragraph; Common Mistakes table
claims.rationale: Docker's best-practices page states that tags may resolve to different underlying versions over time and recommends pinning.

claims.claim_text: Using an init process such as Docker's `--init` or `tini` helps PID 1 reap zombie child processes inside a container.
claims.span_hint: section: 4. GPU Containers, `tini` paragraph; section: 7. Production Readiness, process model paragraphs
claims.rationale: Docker's `docker container run` reference explicitly says `--init` ensures init responsibilities such as reaping zombie processes.

claims.claim_text: Linux namespaces predate Docker, and Docker's workflow took off in 2013 after packaging namespaces, cgroups, and copy-on-write ideas into an easy-to-use toolset.
claims.rationale: The Microsoft Learn article gives concrete dates for Linux namespaces and describes Docker's mid-2013 takeoff built on those primitives.

claims.claim_text: A container can have visible GPU devices yet still report `torch.cuda.is_available()` as false if the PyTorch installation is CPU-only or otherwise not CUDA-enabled.
claims.span_hint: section: 4. GPU Containers, smoke-test and active-learning paragraphs; Common Mistakes table
claims.rationale: PyTorch's installation and verification docs are the right primary source for CUDA-enabled versus CPU-only installs, but the domain is off allowlist; the module's warning is an integration inference from those docs.

further_reading.title: Docker Build Cache Invalidation
further_reading.why_relevant: Explains why Dockerfile ordering and `COPY` placement dominate rebuild time in ML images.

further_reading.title: Networking in Compose
further_reading.why_relevant: Covers default Compose networks, service-name DNS, and why `localhost` inside one container is not another service.

further_reading.title: Configure Liveness, Readiness and Startup Probes
further_reading.why_relevant: Clarifies the readiness-versus-liveness contract that the module applies to ML services.

notes: The two opening 'Why This Module Matters' paragraphs read like composite teaching incidents and should be rewritten as general lessons. Primary-source URLs for NVIDIA and PyTorch claims were found, but both domains are outside the current allowlist, so those claims are marked `needs_allowlist_expansion` rather than forced onto weaker anchors.
